Elsewhere, the Ship Bottom Beach Patrol performed 47 rescues, while 22 rescues occurred at Surf City.

Another active day was reported on the 30th with regards to water rescues along the New Jersey coast as a continued onshore component led to a higher, generally moderate risk of dangerous rip currents. In fact, this last weekend in August, extending from Friday the 28th through Sunday the 30th, proved to be a very busy weekend for area beach patrols with a high number of rescues performed throughout the two to three day stretch. Of note, at 11:30 a.m. EDT on the 30th, a major rip current rescue occurred in Long Beach Township (Ocean County) off 82nd Street. Reports indicated that area lifeguards were outnumbered for a time by the number of victims in the water. As a result, the area police department was called for assistance, and special water rescue equipment was on call.||In addition to this incident, several beach patrols along the New Jersey coast reported numerous rescues occurring from Friday the 28th through Sunday the 30th, with a total of 140 rescues by the Beach Haven Beach Patrol and 42 total rescues by lifeguards at Harvey Cedars during this three day stretch. Also, on Sunday the 30th alone, lifeguards rescued 21 people at Ship Bottom, 7 people at Barnegat Light and 2 people at Surf City, with multiple rescues having previously occurred at these locations on the 28th and 29th.

An area of moderate to heavy rain with isolated thunderstorms moved through New Jersey during the first half of the day on the 20th. On average 1 to 2 inches of rain fell in the southern half of the state, with the highest amounts in the southwest part. This led to some poor drainage as well as pushing over a couple of weak trees and tree limbs. The rain began early on the 20th, fell at its heaviest during the morning commute and ended late in the morning on the 20th. The heavy rain preceded and accompanied the passage of a warm front through New Jersey on the 20th.||The combination of the onshore flow and higher spring astronomical tides associated with the new moon caused minor tidal flooding along the coast and into Delaware Bay and the tidal Delaware River. Minor tidal flooding started in Middlesex and Monmouth Counties with the high tide cycle on the evening of the 19th and morning of the 20th. The most widespread and greatest minor tidal flooding then occurred during the evening and overnight high tide cycle on the 20th. The high tide at Sandy Hook (Monmouth County) reached 7.17 feet above mean lower low water. Minor tidal flooding starts at 6.70 feet above mean lower low water. The high tide at Atlantic City (Atlantic County) reached 6.06 feet above mean lower low water. Minor tidal flooding starts at 6.00 feet above mean lower low water. The high tide at the Cape May Ferry Terminal (Cape May County) reached 6.96 feet above mean lower low water. Minor tidal flooding starts at 6.70 feet above mean lower low water.  The high tide at Reedy Point (New Castle County Delaware), reached 7.32 feet above mean lower low water. Minor tidal flooding starts at 7.20 feet above mean lower low water.  The high tide at Philadelphia just touched the minor tidal flooding level and peaked at 8.21 feet above mean lower low water.||Other gages that reached the threshold for minor tidal flooding included South Amboy (Middlesex County) at 7.83 feet above mean lower low water, Keansburg (Monmouth County) at 7.62 feet above mean lower low water, Belmar (Monmouth County) at 6.58 feet above mean lower low water, the Watson Creek at Manasquan (Monmouth County) at 5.95 feet with a flood stage of 5.5 feet, Barnegat Light (Ocean County) at 3.93 feet above mean lower low water, Absecon (Atlantic County) at 5.60 feet above mean lower low water, Ocean City (Cape May County) at 5.64 feet above mean lower low water, South Dennis (Cape May County) at 6.85 feet above mean lower low water, Sea Isle City (Cape May County) at 5.97 feet above mean lower low water, Avalon (Cape May County) at 5.72 feet above mean lower low water, Stone Harbor (Cape May County) at 6.16 feet above mean lower low water, Cape May Harbor (Cape May County) at 6.32 feet above mean lower low water and Bivalve (Cumberland County) at 7.77 feet above mean lower low water.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain caused poor drainage flooding on the New Jersey Turnpike north of Interchange 2 (U.S. Route 322) in Gloucester County.  Precipitation totals through the morning included 1.88 inches in Mullica Hill, 1.77 inches in Logan Township and 1.40 inches in Beckett.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An approaching cold front produced widely scattered showers and thunderstorms in New Jersey during the afternoon and evening of the 16th. The strongest (severe) thunderstorm occurred in southeast New Jersey during the middle of the afternoon.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A severe thunderstorm knocked down a few trees in Ventor City.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The combination of an unseasonably hot and humid air mass, an approaching cold front and strong winds aloft caused a squall line of severe thunderstorms to move through southern New Jersey during the very late afternoon and early evening of the 23rd. Estimated wind gusts as high as around 85 mph knocked down thousands of trees and caused structural damage to homes and vehicles, mainly from fallen trees. Four direct injuries were reported. Many roadways were closed because of downed trees into the 24th. Hardest hit were Burlington, Camden, Atlantic and in particular Gloucester County. Gloucester County declared a state of emergency. The region suffered approximately 31 million dollars in property damage and was declared a presidential disaster area.  A couple of severe thunderstorms also occurred in northwest New Jersey during the afternoon of the 23rd. While there was one report of a funnel cloud and a waterspout in Barnegat Bay, no confirmed tornadoes occurred.||The severe thunderstorm forced suspension of all PATCO commuter regional rail service in the Philadelphia suburbs as well as the New Jersey Transit Line between Philadelphia and Atlantic City. PATCO service was partially restored on the afternoon of the 24th with full service back on the 25th. Philadelphia to Atlantic City service was not restored until the 26th.||About 410,000 homes and businesses in southern New Jersey lost power.  The 280,000 customers that lost service in the Atlantic City Electric's service area represented a greater number than what occurred during Superstorm Sandy (220,000) and the Derecho of 2012 (206,000). The utility reported that several high transmission lines, five substations, twenty other transmission lines, hundreds of poles as well as 372 incidents of wire damage occurred. The utility received assistance from repair crews as far away as Ohio and New England. About 210,000 of its customers were still without power on the morning of the 24th, 180,000 the afternoon of the 24th, 121,500 the afternoon of the 25th, 84,000 the morning of the 26th and 48,000 the evening of the 26th. Thunderstorms on the 27th slowed restoration efforts and full restoration did not occur until the 30th. Public Service Electric & Gas reported 130,000 of its customers lost power. Full restoration occurred on the 26th. The utility's substations in Collingswood, Maple Shade, Medford and Southampton were damaged. Because of the prolonged power outages, water and ice distribution centers were opened and the American Red Cross assisted with displaced families. ||In Gloucester County, two comfort stations were opened by the Red Cross.  The worst wind damage occurred from Greenwich Township east through Mantua Township. About 11,000 homes and businesses were still without power on the morning of the 28th. In Camden County, the county dispatch office fielded ten times the normal call volume for assistance. The County Public Safety Office fielded 3,522 calls vs 2,432 calls for the entire duration of Superstorm Sandy. Hardest hit was the central part of the county from Gloucester Township east through Cherry Hill and Voorhees Township. In Burlington County, Evesham and Medford Townships were hardest hit.||Crop damage impacted mainly Camden and Gloucester Counties. Most of the initial damage was physical in nature to buildings, facilities and ditches. The impact of the wind on the crops themselves will not be known for months. The lack of hail cut down on the overall crop damage. Verizon cell phone service was also lost in parts of southwest New Jersey on the 23rd and restored by the 24th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The combination of damaging winds and lightning from a line of severe thunderstorms caused slightly more than  half of Cape May County to lose power during the evening of the 23rd. Outages were concentrated from Middle Township north through Ocean City. Power restoration was easier in the county than other places and by noon EDT the next day (24th), the number of customers without power was down to 70.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: After a wet start to the meteorological summer in June, the weather became progressively drier as the summer progressed into September, especially in the northeast part of the state. The United States Drought Monitor reached moderate levels in that part of the state. The New Jersey Department of Environmental Protection issued a drought watch on the 23rd for the northeast part of the state and this included all or parts of  Morris, Hunterdon, Somerset, Middlesex, Monmouth, Mercer and Ocean Counties.||The department urged residents in the affected areas to voluntarily conserve water and for the rest of the state to practice wise water use due to continued dry weather and above-average temperatures.  The drought watch was prompted by continued rainfall deficits that have decreased reservoir, ground water and streamflow levels in the area.  Signs of stress in water supply indicators started to occur.  The department asked residents to use water more carefully and deliberately, especially when it comes to lawn watering and other non-essential uses. Some suggested water conservation tips included: to not over-water lawns and landscaping;  use a hose with a hand-held nozzle to water flowers and shrubs; avoid watering lawns and plants during the heat of the day; use a broom to sweep the sidewalk, rather than a hose; fix leaky faucets and pipes;  turn off the faucet while brushing teeth and shaving and run washing machines and dishwashers only when fully loaded.||Drinking water supply indicators were showing signs of stress from the dry weather and high water demands, including stream flows and ground water levels, as well as declining reservoir storage in the New Jersey Water Supply Authority���s Spruce Run and Manasquan Reservoirs in Hunterdon and Monmouth Counties, respectively. A side effect of the dry weather was an expected smaller (in size) pumpkin crop.  Farmers have had to endure increased costs of water and electricity to irrigate their crops. ||While rainfall amounts in September were closer to normal, monthly precipitation totals in the watch area were all less than normal. This still represented an improvement over July and August when some counties averaged only half their normal rainfall.

A wildfire consumed 1,012 acres of woodland along the Burlington and Ocean County borders from September 7th through the 9th. The wildfire coincided with the last run of 90 degree weather in the state for the year. The Woodmansie Wildfire started in that section of Woodland Township shortly after Noon EDT on the 7th and then spread eastward into Manchester Township in Ocean County. About ninety percent of the acreage that was burned was within this township. The wildfire was contained at Noon EDT on the 9th. ||The wildfire occurred during the last hot spell of the year and what was becoming overall drier conditions. No measurable rain fell in the southern half of New Jersey since August 21st. The high temperature of 94 degrees in Trenton (Mercer County) on the 8th tied a couple of other days during the summer for the highest temperature of the year.  The unseasonably hot start to the month made it easier for the state of New Jersey to have one of its warmest Septembers on record. Since 1895 it was the second warmest September on record for the state with an average temperature of 70.7 degrees (4.9 degrees above normal).  At the Atlantic City International Airport (Atlantic County), the September monthly average temperature of 71.6 degrees (4.4 degrees above normal) was the 6th warmest September on record and in Trenton (Mercer County), the September average temperature of 71.5 degrees was 5.3 degrees above average.

A series of thunderstorms which rode along a wavy cold frontal boundary brought heavy rain into New Jersey on the 10th. The heaviest rain fell along the Interstate 95/295 corridor (especially in Gloucester County) and along coastal sections of New Jersey.  This caused poor drainage as well as some creek flash flooding, mainly in Gloucester County where some event precipitation totals exceeded 5 inches.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain during the middle of the morning caused ponding and flooding of roadways in eastern Monmouth County.  In Ocean Township, portions of Kings Highway and West Park Avenue were closed. In Sea Girt, First Avenue was flooded. Lifeguards assisted with directing traffic around flooded roadways.  In Neptune City, New Jersey State Route 35 and Third Avenue flooded. Flooding was also reported along New Jersey State Route 71 in Deal.  Event precipitation totals included 2.53 inches in Belmar, 2.37 inches in Wall Township, 2.01 inches in Ocean Township and 1.43 inches in Long Branch.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A series of thunderstorms which rode along a wavy cold frontal boundary brought heavy rain into New Jersey on the 10th. The heaviest rain fell along the Interstate 95/295 corridor (especially in Gloucester County) and along coastal sections of New Jersey.  This caused poor drainage as well as some creek flash flooding, mainly in Gloucester County where some event precipitation totals exceeded 5 inches.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A nearly stationary thunderstorm caused flash flooding from Gibbstown (Greenwich Township) through northern East Greenwich Township and Paulsboro. Many roadways were flooded in the area and closed.  Vehicles were stuck in flood waters, but no serious injuries were reported.  Two event precipitation totals  from East Greenwich Township were 4.28 inches and 4.05 inches respectively.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A series of thunderstorms which rode along a wavy cold frontal boundary brought heavy rain into New Jersey on the 10th. EVENT_NARRATIVE: Slow moving thunderstorms caused flash flooding in Mantua Township and Wenonah Borough in the Mantua Creek Basin. In Mantua Township, New Jersey State Route 45 was closed and a few vehicles were trapped in the flood waters.  No serious injuries were reported. Event precipitation totals included 5.79 inches in Wenonah and 2.88 inches in Pitman.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A northeast to east flow around a surface high pressure system combined with spring tides associated with the new moon to produce a higher, generally moderate risk of stronger rip currents along the New Jersey Atlantic shore on the 16th. The rip current risk became so high at Harvey Cedars (Ocean County) that the beach was closed.

The combination of an unseasonably hot and humid air mass, an approaching cold front and strong winds aloft caused a squall line of severe thunderstorms to move through southern New Jersey during the very late afternoon and early evening of the 23rd. Estimated wind gusts as high as around 85 mph knocked down thousands of trees and caused structural damage to homes and vehicles, mainly from fallen trees. Four direct injuries were reported. Many roadways were closed because of downed trees into the 24th. Hardest hit were Burlington, Camden, Atlantic and in particular Gloucester County. Gloucester County declared a state of emergency. The region suffered approximately 31 million dollars in property damage and was declared a presidential disaster area.  A couple of severe thunderstorms also occurred in northwest New Jersey during the afternoon of the 23rd. While there was one report of a funnel cloud and a waterspout in Barnegat Bay, no confirmed tornadoes occurred.||The severe thunderstorm forced suspension of all PATCO commuter regional rail service in the Philadelphia suburbs as well as the New Jersey Transit Line between Philadelphia and Atlantic City. PATCO service was partially restored on the afternoon of the 24th with full service back on the 25th. Philadelphia to Atlantic City service was not restored until the 26th.||About 410,000 homes and businesses in southern New Jersey lost power.  The 280,000 customers that lost service in the Atlantic City Electric's service area represented a greater number than what occurred during Superstorm Sandy (220,000) and the Derecho of 2012 (206,000). The utility reported that several high transmission lines, five substations, twenty other transmission lines, hundreds of poles as well as 372 incidents of wire damage occurred. The utility received assistance from repair crews as far away as Ohio and New England. About 210,000 of its customers were still without power on the morning of the 24th, 180,000 the afternoon of the 24th, 121,500 the afternoon of the 25th, 84,000 the morning of the 26th and 48,000 the evening of the 26th. Thunderstorms on the 27th slowed restoration efforts and full restoration did not occur until the 30th. Public Service Electric & Gas reported 130,000 of its customers lost power. Full restoration occurred on the 26th. The utility's substations in Collingswood, Maple Shade, Medford and Southampton were damaged. Because of the prolonged power outages, water and ice distribution centers were opened and the American Red Cross assisted with displaced families. ||In Gloucester County, two comfort stations were opened by the Red Cross.  The worst wind damage occurred from Greenwich Township east through Mantua Township. About 11,000 homes and businesses were still without power on the morning of the 28th. In Camden County, the county dispatch office fielded ten times the normal call volume for assistance. The County Public Safety Office fielded 3,522 calls vs 2,432 calls for the entire duration of Superstorm Sandy. Hardest hit was the central part of the county from Gloucester Township east through Cherry Hill and Voorhees Township. In Burlington County, Evesham and Medford Townships were hardest hit.||Crop damage impacted mainly Camden and Gloucester Counties. Most of the initial damage was physical in nature to buildings, facilities and ditches. The impact of the wind on the crops themselves will not be known for months. The lack of hail cut down on the overall crop damage. Verizon cell phone service was also lost in parts of southwest New Jersey on the 23rd and restored by the 24th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A severe thunderstorm knocked down multiple trees in Haddonfield Township. Some trees fell across and blocked roadways.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Double barrel cold fronts helped trigger a series of showers and thunderstorms across southern New Jersey during the morning of the 15th. Thunderstorms that were accompanied by very heavy rain caused flash flooding in parts of Salem, Gloucester, Atlantic and Ocean Counties.  The flash flooding in Gloucester and Salem Counties was exacerbated because of the heavy rain and flooding that occurred just the previous morning. Actual rainfall amounts in these locations were as high as 2 to 4 inches, most of which fell during a three hour period.  The combined heavy rain that fell on both the 14th and 15th along the Cumberland and Salem County border also led to river flooding that lasted into the 18th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms with very heavy rain caused poor drainage flash flooding in both Millville and Vineland. In Vineland water 2 to 3 feet deep was reported on roadways. Sections of West Avenue, Plum Street, Chester Avenue and Southwest Boulevard were all flooded and closed.  In Millville, 2nd Street was also flooded. Some roadway flooding was also reported along New Jersey State Route 77 in Upper Deerfield Township. Event precipitation totals included 3.27 inches in Vineland and 2.00 inches in Millville.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms with heavy rain preceding and accompanying a warm front caused flash flooding in adjoining sections of Salem and Cumberland Counties as well as some poor drainage flooding in parts of southern Gloucester, southern Camden and northern Atlantic Counties.  Doppler Radar estimates as well as actual storm total estimates reached 3 to 4 inches where the flash flooding occurred.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Torrential rains from thunderstorms caused flash flooding in Elmer and adjacent parts of Pittsgrove Township. Within the Muddy Run basin, flooding forced the closure of  U.S. Route 40 in Elmer.  Doppler Radar storm total estimates reached 4 inches in and around Elmer.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front over the Finger Lakes region of New York State helped trigger a cluster of showers and thunderstorms with very heavy rain that moved over northwest New Jersey mainly during the evening of the 26th. The heaviest rain fell over western parts of Warren County and especially in far northwest Sussex County. Doppler Radar storm total estimates exceeded 4 inches in the latter.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A cluster of thunderstorms with torrential rain caused poor drainage and small creek flash flooding in the Shimers Brook Basin in Montague Township. Event precipitation totals included 4.74 inches in Montague. A mesonet site within Montague measured 2.57 inches of rain in 70 minutes.

Roadway flooding was reported along Bridgeton Road in the township. Doppler Radar storm total estimates reached 2.5 to 3.0 inches in the northern part of the county. In Pennsville, 2.10 inches of rain fell.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Unseasonably hot and humid weather affected most of New Jersey on the 19th and 20th. High temperatures in most areas reached into the lower to mid 90s both days. The 19th was slightly hotter and more humid overall.   The combination of heat and humidity brought afternoon heat index values as high as 100F to 105F on the 19th. A dissipating cold front on the 20th brought slightly drier air into the region during the afternoon of the 20th and heat index values peaked around 100F . A re-enforcing cold frontal passage on the 21st brought even cooler and drier air into the area and by the 22nd all high temperatures were less than 90 degrees in New Jersey. ||To combat the heat, many counties, cities and municipalities opened cooling centers. The hours of air-conditioned senior citizen centers were extended. In Camden County, free fans were distributed to senior citizens.  A 24-year-old man drowned in the Delaware River as he and two companions were trying to swim to Burlington Island in Burlington County. ||Highest temperatures included 96 degrees in Shamong (Burlington County), 95 degrees in Somerville (Somerset County), Berkeley Township (Ocean County), Hamilton (Mercer County), New Brunswick (Middlesex County) and Hillsborough (Somerset County), 94 degrees in Trenton (Mercer County), Cherry Hill (Camden County), Howell (Monmouth County), Madison (Morris County), Oceanport (Monmouth County) and the Atlantic City International Airport, 93 degrees in Lumberton (Burlington County), West Cape May (Cape May County), Seaside Park (Ocean County), South Harrison (Gloucester County), Pennsville (Salem County), Stewartsville (Warren County), Millville (Cumberland County) and Belmar (Monmouth County), 92 degrees in Hammonton (Atlantic County) and 91 degrees in Wildwood (Cape May County), Flemington (Hunterdon County) and Walpack (Sussex County).

Double barrel cold fronts helped trigger a series of showers and thunderstorms across southern New Jersey during the morning of the 15th. Thunderstorms that were accompanied by very heavy rain caused flash flooding in parts of Salem, Gloucester, Atlantic and Ocean Counties.  The flash flooding in Gloucester and Salem Counties was exacerbated because of the heavy rain and flooding that occurred just the previous morning. Actual rainfall amounts in these locations were as high as 2 to 4 inches, most of which fell during a three hour period.  The combined heavy rain that fell on both the 14th and 15th along the Cumberland and Salem County border also led to river flooding that lasted into the 18th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Runoff from the heavy rain that fell on the mornings of the 14th and 15th caused minor flooding along the Cohansey River on the evening of the 15th. The Cohansey River at Seeley (Hopewell Township) was above its 6 foot flood stage from 622 p.m. EDT through 1133 p.m. EDT on the 15th. It crested at 6.13 feet at 830 p.m. EDT. Two day precipitation totals included 4.46 inches in Upper Deerfield Township.

In Hamilton Township, the Black Horse Pike (U.S. Route 322) was flooded near the Hamilton Mall and Liepzig Avenue. In Northfield, Tilton Road was closed between Hingston and Cresson Roads. In Absecon, widespread occurred along the White Horse Pike (U.S. Route 30). Some vehicles were stuck in the flood waters.  The roadway itself was closed between U.S. Route 9 and County Route 646 (Delilah Road). Shopping center parking lots were turning into lakes. In Galloway Township, the White Horse Pike was also flooded. In Egg Harbor Township, sections of the Black Horse Pike (U.S. Route 322/40) were also flooded.  Elsewhere in the county, some poor drainage flooding also occurred.  Event precipitation totals included 3.85 inches in Galloway Township, 3.02 inches at the Atlantic City International Airport, 2.96 inches in Folsom and 2.20 inches in Hammonton.

Philadelphia to Atlantic City service was not restored until the 26th.||About 410,000 homes and businesses in southern New Jersey lost power.  The 280,000 customers that lost service in the Atlantic City Electric's service area represented a greater number than what occurred during Superstorm Sandy (220,000) and the Derecho of 2012 (206,000). The utility reported that several high transmission lines, five substations, twenty other transmission lines, hundreds of poles as well as 372 incidents of wire damage occurred. The utility received assistance from repair crews as far away as Ohio and New England. About 210,000 of its customers were still without power on the morning of the 24th, 180,000 the afternoon of the 24th, 121,500 the afternoon of the 25th, 84,000 the morning of the 26th and 48,000 the evening of the 26th. Thunderstorms on the 27th slowed restoration efforts and full restoration did not occur until the 30th. Public Service Electric & Gas reported 130,000 of its customers lost power. Full restoration occurred on the 26th. The utility's substations in Collingswood, Maple Shade, Medford and Southampton were damaged. Because of the prolonged power outages, water and ice distribution centers were opened and the American Red Cross assisted with displaced families. ||In Gloucester County, two comfort stations were opened by the Red Cross.  The worst wind damage occurred from Greenwich Township east through Mantua Township. About 11,000 homes and businesses were still without power on the morning of the 28th. In Camden County, the county dispatch office fielded ten times the normal call volume for assistance. The County Public Safety Office fielded 3,522 calls vs 2,432 calls for the entire duration of Superstorm Sandy. Hardest hit was the central part of the county from Gloucester Township east through Cherry Hill and Voorhees Township. In Burlington County, Evesham and Medford Townships were hardest hit.||Crop damage impacted mainly Camden and Gloucester Counties. Most of the initial damage was physical in nature to buildings, facilities and ditches. The impact of the wind on the crops themselves will not be known for months. The lack of hail cut down on the overall crop damage. Verizon cell phone service was also lost in parts of southwest New Jersey on the 23rd and restored by the 24th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A severe thunderstorm caused pockets of wind damage throughout Gloucester Township. Downed trees and wires were reported in Blackwood, Grenloch and Lakeland. In the Grenloch neighborhood, 100-year-old trees were knocked over and telephone poles were snapped.  One downed tree damaged a home and two vehicles in Blackwood.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A moist airmass coupled with daytime heating allowed widely scattered thunderstorms to develop during the afternoon of the 19th. A few thunderstorms produced torrential downpours, leading to localized flash flooding. Doppler radar estimates reached 3 to 4 inches where the flash flooding occurred.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Runoff from earlier rainfall during the afternoon of the 19th caused brief flooding along the Green Brook at Plainfield. The Green Brook at Plainfield was above its 8.0 foot flood stage from 7:43 p.m. EDT through 8:38 p.m. EDT on the 19th, and it crested at 8.14 feet at 8:15 p.m. EDT.

All power was restored by midnight that night.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A mesonet site in Holgate reported a wind gust of 60 mph during a severe thunderstorm.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms with heavy rain preceding and accompanying a warm front caused flash flooding in adjoining sections of Salem and Cumberland Counties as well as some poor drainage flooding in parts of southern Gloucester, southern Camden and northern Atlantic Counties. EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain from thunderstorms fell across extreme southern Gloucester County during the late morning of the 14th. A CoCoRaHS observer measured 2.94 inches of rain.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Unseasonably hot and humid weather affected most of New Jersey on the 19th and 20th. High temperatures in most areas reached into the lower to mid 90s both days. The 19th was slightly hotter and more humid overall.   The combination of heat and humidity brought afternoon heat index values as high as 100F to 105F on the 19th. These were some of the highest heat index values of the entire summer.  A dissipating cold front on the 20th brought slightly drier air into the region during the afternoon of the 20th and heat index values peaked around 100F . A re-enforcing cold frontal passage on the 21st brought even cooler and drier air into the area and by the 22nd all high temperatures were less than 90 degrees in New Jersey. ||To combat the heat, many counties, cities and municipalities opened cooling centers. The hours of air-conditioned senior citizen centers were extended. In Camden County, free fans were distributed to senior citizens. Double barrel cold fronts helped trigger a series of showers and thunderstorms across southern New Jersey during the morning of the 15th. Thunderstorms that were accompanied by very heavy rain caused flash flooding in parts of Salem, Gloucester, Atlantic and Ocean Counties.  The flash flooding in Gloucester and Salem Counties was exacerbated because of the heavy rain and flooding that occurred just the previous morning. Actual rainfall amounts in these locations were as high as 2 to 4 inches, most of which fell during a three hour period.  The combined heavy rain that fell on both the 14th and 15th along the Cumberland and Salem County border also led to river flooding that lasted into the 18th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms with very heavy also caused flash flooding on the barrier islands in Atlantic County. Roadways were closed with vehicles trapped in flash flood waters in Brigantine, Margate and Atlantic City.  In Atlantic City, roadways had up to two feet of water on them and many parking lots were flooded. In Margate City, Ventnor Avenue was flooded and closed. Event precipitation totals included 2.63 inches at the Marina within Atlantic City.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Slow moving thunderstorms that developed behind a departing cold front dropped heavy rain over parts of central and southern Cape May County during the early afternoon on the 6th.  Event precipitation totals reached between 1 and 2 inches and caused some poor drainage flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain caused street flooding in Wildwood as well as along U.S. Route 9 from Cape May Court House south to Rio Grande. Event precipitation totals included 1.51 inches at Cape May Court House, 1.37 inches in Wildwood and 1.15 inches in Beach Creek.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A backdoor cold front triggered showers and thunderstorms with heavy downpours that caused flash flooding across sections of the Raritan Valley in central New Jersey during the evening of the 31st.  Doppler Radar estimates as well as Skywarn spotter reports showed that storm total estimates reached between 2.5 and 5.0 inches across parts of the northern halves of Hunterdon, Somerset and Middlesex Counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms with heavy downpours caused flash flooding in and around North Plainfield.  Vehicles became trapped in high waters on U.S. Route 22 within the township.  Five people were trapped in those vehicles, but no serious injuries were reported.  Event precipitation totals within Somerset County included 2.52 inches in Bernards Township and 2.20 inches in Branchburg Township.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A line of strong to severe thunderstorms that formed in the warm sector of the Susquehanna Valley and moved through southern New Jersey during the first half of the evening on the 9th. It produced some wind damage along its leading edge and then dropped heavy rain that caused some flash flooding later that evening.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain and flash flooding forced the suspension of the commuter train River Line in Camden between the Walter Rand Transportation Center and the Entertainment Center.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A nearly stationary frontal boundary and a low pressure system moving along it combined to develop thunderstorms that produced isolated damaging wind gusts as well as more widespread flash flooding across the southern half of New Jersey during the afternoon and evening of June 1st. Hardest hit with the flash flooding were locations in extreme northern Cumberland County as well as along the Atlantic and Gloucester County borders. Three to six inches of rain fell in this area mainly between 4 p.m. EDT and 10 p.m. EDT on the 1st.  As both the low pressure system and frontal boundary sagged southeast, the severe and heavy rain threat ceased.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The second wave of thunderstorms with very heavy rain caused flash flooding in Vineland. Several roadways were severely flooded within the city.  Event precipitation totals included 4.65 inches in Fairfield Township, 4.60 inches in Vineland, 3.83 inches in Millville, 3.56 inches in Greenwich, 3.10 inches in Newport, 2.98 inches in Upper Deerfield Township and 2.71 inches in Bivalve.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A line of strong to severe thunderstorms formed in the warm sector of the Susquehanna Valley and moved through southern New Jersey during the during the evening on the 9th. In addition, some flash flooding occurred with the line of thunderstorms. About 7,000 homes and businesses lost power in the southern half of New Jersey.  A warm front lifted northeast into central New Jersey during the afternoon of the 9th. This permitted sufficient daytime heating preceding an approaching cold front for thunderstorms to gain strength and severity.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A severe thunderstorm knocked down large tree limbs and wires in Pitman Borough.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: The wind damage from a powerful macroburst maximized across eastern sections of East Greenwich Township. A survey estimated peak wind gusts of 85 mph. Power poles were snapped in the area of East Chawkin Road and Paul Mills Road along Kings Highway (County Route 551) in the Clarksboro section. Shingle damage occurred to numerous homes and sheds were blown over. Many roads were impassable because of downed trees and wires. Wind damage extended to the Mantua Township border through Windsor Way and Carriage Court where one building's roof was completely destroyed. The township estimated 2,800 trees and 100 poles were knocked down.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Unseasonably hot and humid weather affected most of New Jersey on the 19th and 20th. High temperatures in most areas reached into the lower to mid 90s both days. The 19th was slightly hotter and more humid overall.   The combination of heat and humidity brought afternoon heat index values as high as 100F to 105F on the 19th. These were some of the highest heat index values of the entire summer.  A dissipating cold front on the 20th brought slightly drier air into the region during the afternoon of the 20th and heat index values peaked around 100F . A re-enforcing cold frontal passage on the 21st brought even cooler and drier air into the area and by the 22nd all high temperatures were less than 90 degrees in New Jersey. ||To combat the heat, many counties, cities and municipalities opened cooling centers. The hours of air-conditioned senior citizen centers were extended. In Camden County, free fans were distributed to senior citizens. EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A nearly stationary frontal boundary and a low pressure system moving along it combined to develop thunderstorms that produced isolated damaging wind gusts as well as more widespread flash flooding across the southern half of New Jersey during the afternoon and evening of June 1st. Hardest hit with the flash flooding were locations in extreme northern Cumberland County as well as along the Atlantic and Gloucester County borders. Three to six inches of rain fell in this area mainly between 4 p.m. EDT and 10 p.m. EDT on the 1st.  As both the low pressure system and frontal boundary sagged southeast, the severe and heavy rain threat ceased.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The second wave of thunderstorms with very heavy rain caused flash flooding of smaller creeks and roadways in Franklin and Monroe Townships near the border with Atlantic County.  Event precipitation totals included 4.91 inches in Piney Hollow (Franklin Township) and 3.12 inches in South Harrison Township.

Hundreds upon hundreds of trees were either uprooted or snapped. Three people were injured after a tree fell onto their vehicle. Another person was injured after a tree fell on their home on Mississippi Trail.  Several other people were trapped in their homes.  Some gas lines were ruptured. A firefighter suffered cardiac arrest the next day after assisting people through the night.  There were approximately 350 calls for assistance because of wind damage from the thunderstorms within the township.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Unseasonably hot and humid weather affected most of New Jersey on the 19th and 20th. High temperatures in most areas reached into the lower to mid 90s both days. The 19th was slightly hotter and more humid overall.   The combination of heat and humidity brought afternoon heat index values as high as 100F to 105F on the 19th. These were some of the highest heat index values of the entire summer.  A dissipating cold front on the 20th brought slightly drier air into the region during the afternoon of the 20th and heat index values peaked around 100F . A re-enforcing cold frontal passage on the 21st brought even cooler and drier air into the area and by the 22nd all high temperatures were less than 90 degrees in New Jersey. ||To combat the heat, many counties, cities and municipalities opened cooling centers. The hours of air-conditioned senior citizen centers were extended. In Camden County, free fans were distributed to senior citizens.  A 24-year-old man drowned in the Delaware River as he and two companions were trying to swim to Burlington Island in Burlington County. ||Highest temperatures included 96 degrees in Shamong (Burlington County), 95 degrees in Somerville (Somerset County), Berkeley Township (Ocean County), Hamilton (Mercer County), New Brunswick (Middlesex County) and Hillsborough (Somerset County), 94 degrees in Trenton (Mercer County), Cherry Hill (Camden County), Howell (Monmouth County), Madison (Morris County), Oceanport (Monmouth County) and the Atlantic City International Airport, 93 degrees in Lumberton (Burlington County), West Cape May (Cape May County), Seaside Park (Ocean County), South Harrison (Gloucester County), Pennsville (Salem County), Stewartsville (Warren County), Millville (Cumberland County) and Belmar (Monmouth County), 92 degrees in Hammonton (Atlantic County) and 91 degrees in Wildwood (Cape May County), Flemington (Hunterdon County) and Walpack (Sussex County).

The combination of an unseasonably hot and humid air mass, an approaching cold front and strong winds aloft caused a squall line of severe thunderstorms to move through southern New Jersey during the very late afternoon and early evening of the 23rd. Estimated wind gusts as high as around 85 mph knocked down thousands of trees and caused structural damage to homes and vehicles, mainly from fallen trees. Four direct injuries were reported. Many roadways were closed because of downed trees into the 24th. Hardest hit were Burlington, Camden, Atlantic and in particular Gloucester County. Gloucester County declared a state of emergency. The region suffered approximately 31 million dollars in property damage and was declared a presidential disaster area.  A couple of severe thunderstorms also occurred in northwest New Jersey during the afternoon of the 23rd. While there was one report of a funnel cloud and a waterspout in Barnegat Bay, no confirmed tornadoes occurred.||The severe thunderstorm forced suspension of all PATCO commuter regional rail service in the Philadelphia suburbs as well as the New Jersey Transit Line between Philadelphia and Atlantic City. PATCO service was partially restored on the afternoon of the 24th with full service back on the 25th. Philadelphia to Atlantic City service was not restored until the 26th.||About 410,000 homes and businesses in southern New Jersey lost power.  The 280,000 customers that lost service in the Atlantic City Electric's service area represented a greater number than what occurred during Superstorm Sandy (220,000) and the Derecho of 2012 (206,000). The utility reported that several high transmission lines, five substations, twenty other transmission lines, hundreds of poles as well as 372 incidents of wire damage occurred. The utility received assistance from repair crews as far away as Ohio and New England. About 210,000 of its customers were still without power on the morning of the 24th, 180,000 the afternoon of the 24th, 121,500 the afternoon of the 25th, 84,000 the morning of the 26th and 48,000 the evening of the 26th. Thunderstorms on the 27th slowed restoration efforts and full restoration did not occur until the 30th. Public Service Electric & Gas reported 130,000 of its customers lost power. Full restoration occurred on the 26th. The utility's substations in Collingswood, Maple Shade, Medford and Southampton were damaged. Because of the prolonged power outages, water and ice distribution centers were opened and the American Red Cross assisted with displaced families. ||In Gloucester County, two comfort stations were opened by the Red Cross.  The worst wind damage occurred from Greenwich Township east through Mantua Township. About 11,000 homes and businesses were still without power on the morning of the 28th. In Camden County, the county dispatch office fielded ten times the normal call volume for assistance. The County Public Safety Office fielded 3,522 calls vs 2,432 calls for the entire duration of Superstorm Sandy. Hardest hit was the central part of the county from Gloucester Township east through Cherry Hill and Voorhees Township. In Burlington County, Evesham and Medford Townships were hardest hit.||Crop damage impacted mainly Camden and Gloucester Counties. Most of the initial damage was physical in nature to buildings, facilities and ditches. The impact of the wind on the crops themselves will not be known for months. The lack of hail cut down on the overall crop damage. Verizon cell phone service was also lost in parts of southwest New Jersey on the 23rd and restored by the 24th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A severe thunderstorm hit Hammonton Township very hard and caused approximately one million dollars in property damage along with additional damage to the blueberry crop. There were dozens upon dozens of downed and uprooted trees. Trees fell on homes on Bellevue Avenue and French Street. Two buildings at the municipal airport were damaged.  The Hammonton High School roof suffered approximately 100 thousand dollars in wind damage.  The township's overland drip irrigation system that disperses its treated wastewater near the Boyer Avenue Recreational Center was damaged by downed trees. It was estimated that it would take three weeks to clear all of the fallen trees. A mesonet site measured a wind gust of 71 mph in the township.

Because of the prolonged power outages, water and ice distribution centers were opened and the American Red Cross assisted with displaced families. ||In Gloucester County, two comfort stations were opened by the Red Cross.  The worst wind damage occurred from Greenwich Township east through Mantua Township. About 11,000 homes and businesses were still without power on the morning of the 28th. In Camden County, the county dispatch office fielded ten times the normal call volume for assistance. The County Public Safety Office fielded 3,522 calls vs 2,432 calls for the entire duration of Superstorm Sandy. Hardest hit was the central part of the county from Gloucester Township east through Cherry Hill and Voorhees Township. In Burlington County, Evesham and Medford Townships were hardest hit.||Crop damage impacted mainly Camden and Gloucester Counties. Most of the initial damage was physical in nature to buildings, facilities and ditches. The impact of the wind on the crops themselves will not be known for months. The lack of hail cut down on the overall crop damage. Verizon cell phone service was also lost in parts of southwest New Jersey on the 23rd and restored by the 24th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A Skywarn Spotter estimated a wind gust of 60 mph as a severe thunderstorm moved through Swedesboro Borough.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Unseasonably hot and humid weather affected most of New Jersey on the 19th and 20th. High temperatures in most areas reached into the lower to mid 90s both days. The 19th was slightly hotter and more humid overall.   The combination of heat and humidity brought afternoon heat index values as high as 100F to 105F on the 19th. These were some of the highest heat index values of the entire summer.  A dissipating cold front on the 20th brought slightly drier air into the region during the afternoon of the 20th and heat index values peaked around 100F . A re-enforcing cold frontal passage on the 21st brought even cooler and drier air into the area and by the 22nd all high temperatures were less than 90 degrees in New Jersey. ||To combat the heat, many counties, cities and municipalities opened cooling centers. The hours of air-conditioned senior citizen centers were extended. In Camden County, free fans were distributed to senior citizens.  A 24-year-old man drowned in the Delaware River as he and two companions were trying to swim to Burlington Island in Burlington County. ||Highest temperatures included 96 degrees in Shamong (Burlington County), 95 degrees in Somerville (Somerset County), Berkeley Township (Ocean County), Hamilton (Mercer County), New Brunswick (Middlesex County) and Hillsborough (Somerset County), 94 degrees in Trenton (Mercer County), Cherry Hill (Camden County), Howell (Monmouth County), Madison (Morris County), Oceanport (Monmouth County) and the Atlantic City International Airport, 93 degrees in Lumberton (Burlington County), West Cape May (Cape May County), Seaside Park (Ocean County), South Harrison (Gloucester County), Pennsville (Salem County), Stewartsville (Warren County), Millville (Cumberland County) and Belmar (Monmouth County), 92 degrees in Hammonton (Atlantic County) and 91 degrees in Wildwood (Cape May County), Flemington (Hunterdon County) and Walpack (Sussex County).
Dense fog developed during a busy travel day, resulting in impacts to travel.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Teterboro airport reported visibility of 1/4 mile or less in the morning. This poor visibility impacted travel during a busy travel day after the holiday.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: After a return to closer to normal precipitation in October, the month of November proved to be drier than normal across much of New Jersey, and the United States Drought Monitor continued abnormally dry to moderate drought levels in northern to northeast New Jersey. The New Jersey's Department of Environmental Protection also continued the drought watch for the northern to northeast part of the state, and this included all or parts of Morris, Hunterdon, Somerset, Middlesex, Monmouth, Mercer and Ocean Counties. In addition, water restrictions that were imposed during October in Newton (Sussex County) continued because the water level at Morris Lake, the town's reservoir in Sparta, was dropping. The restrictions prohibited watering of lawns and gardens; washing cars, trucks, boats or any recreational vehicle; washing driveways, sidewalks or walkways and filling swimming pools or ornamental pools. Declining reservoir storage noted in October in the New Jersey Water Supply Authority���s Spruce Run and Manasquan Reservoirs in Hunterdon and Monmouth Counties, also persisted in November. Another noticeable and agricultural side effect of the dry weather was an expected smaller (in size) pumpkin crop. Farmers had to endure increased costs of water and electricity to irrigate their crops. ||The Department of Environmental Protection urged residents in the drought watch area to voluntarily conserve water as precipitation deficits were greater in northern New Jersey and for the rest of the state to practice wise water use due to overall continued dry weather and above-average temperatures. The drought watch was continued due to below average precipitation that has decreased reservoir, ground water, and streamflow levels in the area. Signs of stress in water supply indicators persisted. The department asked residents to use water more carefully and deliberately, especially when it comes to lawn watering and other non-essential uses. The goal is to moderate water demand through voluntary conservation. Some suggested water conservation tips included: to not over-water lawns and landscaping; use a hose with a hand-held nozzle to water flowers and shrubs; avoid watering lawns and plants during the heat of the day; use a broom to sweep the sidewalk, rather than a hose; fix leaky faucets and pipes; turn off the faucet while brushing teeth and shaving and run washing machines and dishwashers only when fully loaded.

An area of low pressure along the Middle Atlantic coast produced a couple of strong easterly wind gusts along the coast of New Jersey during the morning of the 10th. A 40 mph wind gust was measured at Cape May (Cape May County) and at Ocean City (Cape May County). The low pressure slowly tracked along the Eastern Seaboard from around the Tidewater of Virginia early in the day, crossing the lower Delmarva Peninsula and eventually emerging off the southern New Jersey coast by the evening. In addition to some strong wind gusts, this coastal area of low pressure also produced rainfall amounts ranging mostly between 1.0 inches to 2.50 inches of rain in New Jersey.

A mixture of frozen precipitation occurred from the evening of the 28th into the morning of the 29th over the higher terrain of northwest New Jersey. Some slippery conditions and traveling difficulties were reported in portions of Sussex, Warren, and Morris Counties as a result of a combination of sleet, freezing rain, and some snow that fell across the area before warmer air arrived and changed all precipitation over to rain by mid to late morning on the 29th. ||The mixture of frozen precipitation was caused in part by a low pressure system that tracked from the mid-Mississippi Valley on the afternoon of the 28th to the Great Lakes by the morning of the 29th. Its associated warm front lifted north toward New Jersey and gradually began to dislodge cold air at the surface that had been in place as a result of strong Canadian high pressure extending down across the northern Mid-Atlantic states. While some snow did occur, most of the frozen precipitation was in the form of sleet and then freezing rain as a warm layer of air expanded aloft and cold, sub-freezing air gradually eroded away at the surface. By mid to late morning on the 29th, surface temperatures were above freezing and precipitation had transitioned to rain. ||Storm total snow and sleet amounts included 1.2 inches in Wantage (Sussex County); 0.8 inches in Montague Township (Sussex County); 0.6 inches in Andover Township (Sussex County) and in Rockaway Township (Morris County); 0.5 inches in Hardyston Township (Sussex County) and in Delaware (Warren County) and also in Jefferson Township (Morris County) and in Mine Hill Township (Morris County); 0.4 inches in Randolph Township (Morris County); 0.3 inches in Roxbury Township, Green Pond, and Marcella (Morris County); and 0.1 inches in Greenwich Township and Hackettstown (Warren County).||Storm total ice accretion as a result of freezing rain included 0.09 inches at the Sussex Airport (Sussex County), 0.01 inches in Denville Township (Morris County), and a trace recorded in Wantage Township and in High Point (Sussex County).

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: After a drier than normal November and a dry first half of December across much of New Jersey, the United States Drought Monitor continued abnormally dry to moderate drought levels in northern to northeast New Jersey. The New Jersey's Department of Environmental Protection also continued the drought watch for the northern to northeast part of the state, and this included all or parts of Morris, Hunterdon, Somerset, Middlesex, Monmouth, Mercer and Ocean Counties.
